does: This paper analyzes the reward hacking problem in text-to-image diffusion models, where optimizing for human preference rewards causes models to sacrifice diversity in generated images. The authors prove reward hacking is inevitable and evaluate standard regularization approaches like KL divergence and LoRA scaling, finding they have fundamental limitations for diffusion models. They propose Annealed Importance Guidance (AIG), an inference-time regularization technique inspired by Annealed Importance Sampling that operates during generation rather than training. AIG maintains the base model's diversity while achieving Pareto-optimal tradeoffs between reward maximization and output diversity, demonstrated empirically across multiple experimental settings.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of aligning text-to-image models with human preferences through reward modeling and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), methods that have been successful in language models but present novel challenges in diffusion models. It extends prior research on mode collapse and diversity loss in generative models by specifically characterizing the reward hacking phenomenon in the diffusion setting. The paper relates to earlier work on inference-time guidance mechanisms in diffusion models and connects to broader research on balancing performance with diversity in reward-optimized systems, including theoretical frameworks for understanding Pareto tradeoffs in machine learning.
enables: This work opens avenues for developing more sophisticated guidance mechanisms that balance alignment with diversity in generative models. It suggests that inference-time regularization may be a more viable path than training-time approaches for managing reward-diversity tradeoffs, potentially inspiring similar techniques for other domains like text generation or video synthesis. The theoretical characterization of inevitable reward hacking could inform the design of more robust reward functions and preference datasets. Future work could explore adaptive annealing schedules, learned guidance weights, or connections to other sampling techniques like rejection sampling, enabling safer deployment of preference-optimized generative models in high-stakes applications.
